event: The Purification of the Virgin (Presentation at the Temple)
symbols: temple steps — Mary's ascent to fulfill the Law, the threshold between old and new covenants
high priest at the top — the Levitical order receiving the one who bears the new High Priest
Mary in white veil — ritual purity and obedience to the Mosaic law
Gothic temple architecture — the Church as the new temple
gathered crowd — Israel witnessing the fulfillment of the Law
description: A richly detailed illuminated manuscript page showing the Purification of the Virgin at the Temple. A grand Gothic-style temple dominates the center with ornate arched colonnades and stairs. At the top of the steps, a high priest in ceremonial robes receives or blesses figures ascending toward him. Mary, in a white veil and blue-gold robe, ascends the broad stone stairs at center. Below, a crowd of onlookers in varied colored garments watches from the left. At the lower right, a figure in white robes (possibly Simeon or another priest) stands apart.
theological_description: The Limbourg Brothers depict the Presentation of Christ at the Temple / Purification of the Virgin, as narrated in Luke 2:22-38. According to the Mosaic Law (Leviticus 12:2-8), a woman was to present herself for purification forty days after giving birth to a male child. Simultaneously, every firstborn male was to be consecrated to the Lord (Exodus 13:2, 12). Mary and Joseph brought Jesus to the Temple in Jerusalem to fulfill both requirements, offering 'a pair of doves or two young pigeons' — the sacrifice of the poor (Luke 2:24, Leviticus 12:8). At the Temple, the aged Simeon, moved by the Holy Spirit, recognized the infant as the promised Messiah and proclaimed: 'My eyes have seen your salvation, which you have prepared in the sight of all nations: a light for revelation to the Gentiles, and the glory of your people Israel' (Luke 2:30-32). This event reveals the paradox of God submitting to his own Law: the Creator presenting himself as a redeemed firstborn, the sinless mother undergoing purification. It also marks the moment when the old covenant recognizes the new — Simeon, representing faithful Israel, acknowledges that the long-awaited consolation has arrived.

scripture_refs.reason: Mary and Joseph bring Jesus to the Temple for purification and the presentation of the firstborn
Simeon receives the child and proclaims the Nunc Dimittis — a light for the Gentiles
The Mosaic law of purification after childbirth that Mary fulfills
Consecration of every firstborn male to the Lord — the law fulfilled by presenting Jesus
The Lord whom you seek will suddenly come to his temple — fulfilled when the infant Christ enters
